invariant_definition: The doctrinal position that *bheda* and *abheda* between *brahman* and the world (especially between *brahman* and *jīva*) are equally and simultaneously real. The earliest known proponent is Bhartṛprapañca (cited but not extant); Bhāskara (8th–9th c.) is the principal classical exponent; Yādava-Prakāśa (Rāmānuja's teacher), Nimbārka (Dvaitādvaita as a variant), Caitanya / Jīva Gosvāmī (Acintya-Bhedābheda as the developed Vaiṣṇava form). The position is structurally distinct from Advaita (which makes *abheda* alone real) and from Dvaita (which makes *bheda* alone real). Schools differ on whether the simultaneity is rationally articulable (Bhāskara: yes, via real *upādhi*-borne *pariṇāma*) or is *acintya*, beyond logical resolution (Caitanya tradition).

per_school.definition: *Bheda* arises from real *upādhi*-borne *pariṇāma*; *abheda* obtains in the underlying substrate. *Brahman* really transforms into the world through *upādhi*-conditions; the world is therefore really one with *brahman* (in substrate) and really different (in *upādhi*-condition).
*Bheda* and *abheda* between *brahman* and *jīva* / *jagat* are both natural (*svābhāvika*) — neither derived from the other, both equally primary. *Brahman* is identified with Kṛṣṇa.

*Bheda* and *abheda* between Kṛṣṇa and his *śakti*s (including *jīva*s) obtain simultaneously and *acintya* — beyond logical resolution. The relation is real but its logical structure exceeds reason; *śabda* is the only *pramāṇa* for it.

The user's thesis identifies the *bhedābheda* trajectory (Bhartṛprapañca → Bhāskara → Madhva → Caitanya) as a self-correcting recovery of the realism implicit in the *Brahma-Sūtra* — see T2 of the position-brief. *Bhedābheda* is thus structurally central to the user's position, not a peripheral school.

translator_note: 'Difference-and-non-difference' is the standard scholarly translation and is correct. The doctrine has no clean Western analogue; the closest is perhaps Spinoza's *Deus sive natura* with the substance-mode relation, but *bhedābheda* refuses Spinoza's modal-reduction of the world to substance.
